doomsday_clock.context: Closest to midnight in the Clock's 79-year history. Primary drivers: nuclear risk (including expired arms-control architecture), climate change, and disruptive technologies.

overall_summary.framing: Multiple systems show building stress rather than a single acute crisis. Nuclear risk remains the dominant Doomsday Clock driver after New START expired with no successor. A strong El Niño trajectory is amplifying heat, drought, fire, and food-price pressure. Solar Cycle activity produced one of the strongest radiation storms in decades in early 2026. Several large earthquakes (including M7.8 Mindanao) underline ongoing seismic exposure.
overall_summary.cumulative_context: Quiet intervals do not erase multi-month pressure across nuclear posture, food systems, energy and logistics, geomagnetic vulnerability, and extreme weather. The profile is systemic accumulation, not isolated spikes.

categories.name: Seismic Activity
categories.trend: Elevated large-event year; no global rate anomaly
categories.cumulative_context: 2026 has already produced multiple M7+ events, including a destructive M7.8 in the Philippines and a major Venezuela sequence. Global background rates remain within long-term norms, but populated-region exposure was demonstrated.
categories.metrics: Multiple M7+ events in H1 2026 (USGS). Background ~4–6 M5+ events/day globally.

recent_events.description: M7.3 earthquake offshore southern Mexico (Chiapas region)
recent_events.impact: Strong shaking reported; brief tsunami warning later cancelled; aftershocks to ~M6

recent_events.description: M7.5 earthquake near Yumare, Venezuela (significant sequence)
recent_events.impact: Major shaking in northern Venezuela; reported as among the year's most consequential events

recent_events.description: M7.8 Mindanao (Philippines) earthquake — strongest of 2026 to date
recent_events.impact: Violent shaking (up to IX); substantial regional impact; tsunami monitoring activated

recent_events.description: M7.5 earthquake west of Neiafu, Tonga
recent_events.impact: Deep/intermediate event; tsunami evaluation performed

recent_events.description: Additional M7+ events including near Japan (Miyako region) and Indonesia
recent_events.impact: Regional alerts and infrastructure checks; limited global cascade

categories.sources_compared: USGS Earthquake Hazards Program + EMSC + PHIVOLCS + GFZ
categories.discrepancies: High agreement on magnitudes and locations. Casualty and damage figures can lag or vary by national reporting.
categories.conservative_reading: 2026 is an active large-event year in several arcs, but this does not by itself indicate a global rate change. Local preparedness remains the primary mitigation.

cross_references.connection: Large events in populated zones can rapidly raise reconstruction and insurance costs
cross_references.metric: Mindanao and Venezuela sequences as 2026 examples

categories.prep_priority: Know your local seismic risk, secure heavy furniture, and maintain water/meds for 72+ hours in high-hazard zones.

categories.name: Volcanic Activity
categories.trend: Regional unrest elevated; global background otherwise typical
categories.cumulative_context: No single global volcanic crisis, but submarine and Pacific Ring of Fire unrest has produced repeated aviation and coastal alerts in 2026.
categories.metrics: Multiple volcanoes at elevated alert levels worldwide (Smithsonian/USGS weekly reports).

recent_events.description: Submarine volcanic activity off New Zealand triggering tsunami warnings (later downgraded)
recent_events.impact: Coastal evacuations and temporary disruption; warnings later relaxed

recent_events.description: Increased unrest and ash advisories at several Pacific Ring of Fire volcanoes
recent_events.impact: Aviation ash advisories and flight reroutes

categories.sources_compared: Smithsonian/USGS Weekly Volcanic Activity Report + GeoNet + VAAC
categories.discrepancies: Good alignment on major unrest; timing of alert level changes can differ by agency.
categories.conservative_reading: Treat regional alerts as authoritative. Global activity is not extreme, but aviation and coastal exposure is real.

cross_references.connection: Ash and tsunami alerts disrupt aviation and coastal logistics
cross_references.metric: NZ event and Pacific ash advisories

categories.prep_priority: If you live near active volcanoes or tsunami coasts, follow official alert channels and keep an evacuation plan.

categories.name: Solar & Space Weather
categories.cumulative_context: Post-solar-maximum activity remains capable of severe storms. January 2026 produced an S4 radiation storm (strongest in decades) and a G4 geomagnetic storm; further X-class flaring and G3 activity followed.
categories.metrics: Jan 2026 S4 radiation storm; G4 geomagnetic storm; multiple early-Feb X-class flares; June–July 2026 G3 storm with widespread auroras.

recent_events.description: G3 geomagnetic storm sequence; auroras reported across ~30 U.S. states around early July
recent_events.impact: Elevated satellite and HF radio impacts; public aurora visibility at mid-latitudes

recent_events.description: Six X-class solar flares in four days
recent_events.impact: Continued high-energy flare environment after January storm

recent_events.description: X1.9 flare → unusually fast CME → S4 solar radiation storm (strongest since Oct 1989) and G4 geomagnetic storm
recent_events.impact: Severe radiation environment for polar aviation/satellites; auroras as far south as Spain and Arizona; tech anomaly risk elevated

recent_events.description: Major solar storm with auroras observed as far south as Central Mexico
recent_events.impact: Demonstrated continued high-latitude and mid-latitude coupling

categories.sources_compared: NOAA SWPC + NASA + Solen + contemporaneous news corroboration
categories.discrepancies: Storm class (G/S/R) is standardized; impacts on specific satellites or grids are often under-reported publicly.
categories.conservative_reading: 2026 has already shown that severe space weather is not theoretical. Grid and satellite operators should treat G3–G4 / S3–S4 as planning cases, not edge cases.

cross_references.connection: Weaker field / expanding SAA increase coupling of solar storms into infrastructure
cross_references.metric: Higher GIC and satellite radiation exposure risk

cross_references.connection: Severe storms can affect satellites, aviation routes, and power systems
cross_references.metric: Jan 2026 S4/G4 as recent stress test

categories.prep_priority: Operators: maintain space-weather playbooks. Public: expect possible GPS/HF and rare power anomalies during major storms; follow utility guidance.

categories.name: Ocean Temperatures & Marine Heatwaves
categories.trend: Worsening with strengthening El Niño trajectory
categories.cumulative_context: El Niño strengthened through mid-2026 with elevated probability of a very strong event later in the year, driving marine heat and downstream drought/food pressure.
categories.metrics: Niño-3.4 elevated; CPC/IRI outlooks previously assigned high odds of strong late-2026 El Niño conditions.

recent_events.description: Strengthening El Niño and expanding Pacific marine heat signals
recent_events.impact: Coral bleaching risk, fishery stress, early drought and food-price pressure in vulnerable regions

recent_events.description: Significant marine heatwave development in central/eastern Pacific
recent_events.impact: Early ecological stress indicators

categories.sources_compared: NOAA CPC + IRI Columbia + BOM + Coral Reef Watch
categories.discrepancies: Peak intensity forecasts differ slightly; direction of risk is consistent.
categories.conservative_reading: Treat El Niño as a multi-season risk to food, water, and coastal ecosystems — not a single-month weather story.

cross_references.connection: Shared driver of heat and drought probability
Food price and agricultural income impacts

categories.prep_priority: In vulnerable regions: water storage, crop planning, and household food buffers.

categories.name: Sea Level Rise & Coastal Extremes
categories.trend: Long-term rise continues; acute extremes episodic
categories.cumulative_context: Global mean sea level continues upward (~3.5–4 mm/yr class). King tides plus higher baseline produce more frequent nuisance flooding even without major storms.
categories.metrics: Long-term GMSL rise ongoing (satellite altimetry era).

recent_events.description: King-tide and high-baseline combinations producing minor coastal flooding in low-lying areas
recent_events.impact: Temporary road closures and property impacts

categories.sources_compared: NOAA + UH Sea Level Center + CSIRO
categories.discrepancies: Rate estimates are consistent at multi-year scale.
categories.conservative_reading: Adaptation and insurance are the practical responses; sudden global step-change is not indicated.

cross_references.connection: Repeated flooding raises maintenance and insurance costs

categories.prep_priority: Coastal residents: review flood exposure maps and insurance; support local adaptation planning.

categories.name: Weather Extremes, Drought & Heat Stress
categories.trend: Worsening — early-2026 extremes already severe in multiple regions
categories.cumulative_context: Record heat and drought signals appeared early in 2026 across the Southern Hemisphere and parts of the U.S., consistent with a warm baseline plus El Niño influence.
categories.metrics: Multi-region heat records; U.S. drought coverage peaked near 69% of CONUS in Jan 2026 (U.S. Drought Monitor).

recent_events.description: Record-breaking heatwave in the U.S. West threatening snowpack and early fire-season conditions
recent_events.impact: Accelerated melt risk and elevated wildfire prep concerns

recent_events.description: Southern Hemisphere extreme heat and fire complex: Australia heat dome (temps near 50°C), Chile coastal fires (reported fatalities), Patagonia fires, South Africa severe fire activity
recent_events.impact: Deaths, evacuations, power stress, and large burned areas under weak La Niña / warm baseline

recent_events.description: U.S. Drought Monitor: ~69% of the United States under drought conditions
recent_events.impact: Agricultural and water-resource stress heading into the year

categories.sources_compared: NOAA / WMO / U.S. Drought Monitor / Reuters and national met services
categories.discrepancies: Local records are firm; attribution of any single event always has uncertainty bands.
categories.conservative_reading: Heat and drought are already operational risks in 2026, not only long-term projections.

cross_references.connection: Heat + drought prime fuels
cross_references.metric: Early-season acreage and Southern Hemisphere fires

cross_references.connection: El Niño amplifies many land extremes

categories.prep_priority: Heat action plans, hydration, cooling access, and agricultural water planning.

categories.name: Wildfire Activity & Air Quality
categories.trend: Elevated early-season and Southern Hemisphere activity
categories.cumulative_context: Early 2026 saw severe Southern Hemisphere fire impacts and an aggressive start to parts of the North American season under widespread drought.
categories.metrics: U.S. 2026 season already multi-million acres by mid-year class reporting; AccuWeather and NIFC outlooks flagged elevated national potential.

recent_events.description: U.S. wildfire season running ahead of average acreage in multiple assessments; drought-primed fuels
recent_events.impact: Early large fires (including Plains events), air-quality impacts, evacuations

recent_events.description: Severe fires in Chile (coastal communities, reported deaths), Argentina Patagonia, South Africa, and fire weather with extreme Australian heat
recent_events.impact: Fatalities, community destruction, regional air-quality crises

recent_events.description: Western U.S. heatwave raising late-season fire risk via snowpack loss and fuel drying
recent_events.impact: Forward risk elevation for summer/fall

categories.sources_compared: NIFC + NASA FIRMS / GWIS + national reporting + Reuters
categories.discrepancies: Acreage totals revise through the season; fatality counts depend on official tallies.
categories.conservative_reading: Treat 2026 as a high-attention fire year in drought-affected regions. Air quality is a public-health pathway even far from flames.

cross_references.connection: Primary physical drivers
Suppression costs, insurance, transport and health impacts

categories.prep_priority: Defensible space, go-bags, N95/filtration for smoke, and heed evacuation orders early.

categories.name: Military Conflict & Escalation Risks
categories.trend: Sustained high tension; nuclear-adjacent rhetoric persists
categories.cumulative_context: Major conflicts remain active. Nuclear signaling related to the Russia–Ukraine war has been a multi-year feature; European and Indo-Pacific flashpoints keep escalation pathways open.
categories.metrics: Ongoing high-intensity wars; repeated nuclear rhetoric tracked by research groups; arms-control architecture degraded.

recent_events.description: Sustained Russian nuclear signaling throughout the Ukraine war (research dashboards track peaks in rhetoric and doctrine shifts)
recent_events.impact: Elevated miscalculation risk; shapes alliance and arms-control behavior

recent_events.description: Russian nuclear doctrine updates and continued public nuclear threats reported by Western governments and research orgs
recent_events.impact: Normalized nuclear coercion narratives

recent_events.description: Multiple active high-intensity conflicts with energy and food market spill-overs
recent_events.impact: Persistent commodity and humanitarian pressure

categories.sources_compared: CFR + ACLED + ISW + Carnegie + parliamentary research
categories.discrepancies: Escalation probability estimates differ by analyst; factual occurrence of rhetoric and fighting is not in dispute.
categories.conservative_reading: Conflict risk is not only local — it feeds nuclear posture, energy prices, and food security.

cross_references.connection: Active wars involving nuclear-armed states raise use and accident pathways
Energy and grain market volatility

categories.prep_priority: Follow reputable conflict trackers; expect energy/food price volatility; avoid amplifying unverified escalation claims.

categories.name: Near-Earth Objects (NEOs)
categories.trend: Stable — no significant near-term impact threat
categories.cumulative_context: Survey completeness for large objects is high. No object currently carries a meaningful short-term impact probability of concern.
categories.metrics: No high-probability Sentry impacts in the next 90 days; close approaches at safe distances continue routinely.

recent_events.description: Multiple small NEO close approaches at safe distances
recent_events.impact: Scientific observation opportunities only

categories.sources_compared: NASA CNEOS Close Approach + Sentry tables
categories.discrepancies: None material on current risk list.
categories.conservative_reading: Continue support for surveys; no public action required for impact risk at present.

categories.name: Geomagnetic Field Stability & Core Dynamics
categories.trend: Long-term weakening and rapid pole drift continue
categories.cumulative_context: Field intensity has declined on the order of ~10% since systematic measurements; North magnetic pole drift remains rapid; South Atlantic Anomaly expansion increases satellite radiation exposure.
categories.metrics: Multi-decadal intensity decline; pole drift tens of km/year; SAA growth documented by Swarm and NOAA models.

recent_events.description: Continued rapid pole movement and SAA expansion in satellite-era models
recent_events.impact: Higher cumulative radiation dose for LEO satellites and some aviation routes; slightly higher GIC sensitivity during storms

categories.sources_compared: ESA Swarm + NOAA/NCEI + USGS magnetic models
categories.discrepancies: Agreement on observed change; disagreement on proximity to a full reversal (none is imminent on human timescales in mainstream models).
categories.conservative_reading: Not a doomsday pole-flip story. It is a slow resilience issue for grids and satellites when combined with strong solar storms.

cross_references.connection: Weaker field amplifies storm impacts
cross_references.metric: Jan 2026 severe storm under a weakened field

cross_references.connection: Long-term grid and satellite hardening costs

categories.prep_priority: Infrastructure operators: include geomagnetic scenarios in resilience planning.

categories.name: Infrastructure & Financial Strain
categories.trend: Worsening under stacked climate, conflict, and space-weather stress
categories.cumulative_context: Food prices, disaster losses, energy volatility, and insurance stress accumulate even when no single headline crisis dominates a given week.
categories.metrics: Elevated food-price pressure under El Niño path; early-season fire and heat losses; space-weather operational anomalies possible after major storms.

recent_events.description: Stacked impacts from Southern Hemisphere fires/heat, U.S. drought and early wildfires, and Pacific climate trajectory
recent_events.impact: Agricultural and household cost pressure; disaster response load

recent_events.description: Severe solar radiation and geomagnetic storm sequence as an operational stress test for satellites and high-latitude systems
recent_events.impact: Anomaly risk and contingency activation for operators

categories.sources_compared: FAO + Fed supply-chain indicators + disaster agencies + SWPC
categories.discrepancies: Macro indicators lag local lived experience; insurance and fiscal impacts emerge over quarters.
categories.conservative_reading: Resilience is about buffers and redundancy, not predicting the next single shock.

cross_references.connection: Food and agriculture pathway
Energy and grain markets
Satellite and grid pathways

categories.prep_priority: Household cash/food/water buffers; reduce single points of failure; follow official disaster accounts.

categories.name: Nuclear Risk
categories.trend: Elevated and structurally worse after arms-control erosion
categories.cumulative_context: Doomsday Clock at 85 seconds. New START expired in February 2026 with no successor treaty, removing the last bilateral U.S.–Russia strategic arms limits. Modernization continues; nuclear rhetoric around Ukraine has been persistent for years.
categories.metrics: Clock at 85s (Jan 2026). New START expired Feb 2026. Ongoing arsenal modernization (SIPRI/BAS). Multi-year nuclear signaling in the Russia–Ukraine war.

recent_events.description: New START — the last U.S.–Russia bilateral strategic arms control treaty — expires with no replacement in force
recent_events.impact: No mutually verified limits on deployed strategic warheads/delivery systems between the two largest arsenals

recent_events.description: Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists sets Doomsday Clock to 85 seconds to midnight
recent_events.impact: Closest setting in Clock history; nuclear risk cited as a primary driver alongside climate and disruptive tech

recent_events.description: Russian nuclear doctrine adjustments and continued nuclear threat rhetoric tracked through the Ukraine war
recent_events.impact: Sustained coercion signaling and alliance counter-posturing

recent_events.description: Continued nuclear force modernization across major nuclear-armed states
recent_events.impact: Higher readiness and diversity of delivery systems over time

categories.sources_compared: Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists + SIPRI + Carnegie + parliamentary research + SWP
categories.discrepancies: Analysts differ on use probability; agreement is strong that structural risk (arsenals + doctrine + eroded arms control + active wars) is high.
categories.conservative_reading: Nuclear risk is the leading systemic catastrophe pathway on this dashboard. Public prep is inherently limited; diplomatic and arms-control repair are the meaningful levers.

cross_references.connection: Wars involving nuclear-armed states are the primary near-term pathway to nuclear use
Any nuclear use would dominate all other infrastructure/financial scenarios

categories.prep_priority: Support verified arms control and de-escalation. Personally: know official civil-defense guidance in your country; do not rely on social-media nuclear rumors.
